metadata.summary: GitOps uses Git as single source of truth for declarative infrastructure. Pull-based operators (ArgoCD, FluxCD) reconcile live state with Git, providing drift detection and audit trails.

content.title: GitOps
content.summary: GitOps uses a Git repository as the single source of truth for declarative infrastructure and applications. A pull-based operator (ArgoCD, FluxCD) running inside the cluster continuously reconciles live state with the desired state in Git, providing automatic drift detection and correction. The recommended pattern is hybrid: CI pipeline builds and pushes image tags / manifest updates to Git; the GitOps agent pulls and applies. Use folders (not branches) for environment separation.

why.p: Pull-based GitOps eliminates cluster credentials from CI pipelines, creates an immutable audit trail of every change, and enables automatic drift correction without manual intervention. 90%+ of Kubernetes deployments now use GitOps (2025 data); 80%+ of adopters report higher reliability. The audit trail satisfies compliance requirements that push-based pipelines cannot easily provide.

list.item: Kubernetes-native deployments requiring drift detection and automatic reconciliation.
Multi-cluster/multi-environment setups where consistency must be enforced.
Compliance or audit requirements where every config change needs a Git trail.
Progressive delivery (canary/blue-green) with automated metric-driven promotion.
Non-containerized legacy systems — GitOps tooling is Kubernetes-native.
Teams with no Kubernetes experience — the learning curve adds cost before value.
Tight deployment sequencing needs where push-based CI gives finer control.
Small teams (<5 engineers) with a single environment and simple deploy pipeline.

section.name: core-principles
section.p: Four OpenGitOps principles form the foundation of GitOps practice.

list.item: Declarative: infrastructure and applications defined as code in YAML/JSON
Versioned and immutable: all configuration stored in Git with full history
Pulled automatically: agents inside clusters pull changes from Git (not pushed by CI)
Continuously reconciled: automatic drift detection and correction without manual triggers

section.name: pull-vs-push-model
section.p: Understanding the trade-offs between pull-based and push-based deployment models is critical for GitOps success.

rule.statement: Prefer pull-based GitOps for security-focused, multi-cluster, or compliance-driven environments.
rule.rationale: Pull-based (ArgoCD, FluxCD) eliminates cluster credentials from CI pipelines — the agent inside the cluster only needs read access to the Git repo. Push-based requires exposing cluster API credentials to external CI systems, which must be rotated and audited separately.

rule.statement: Use the hybrid model (CI pushes manifest updates to Git, GitOps agent applies) for most new deployments.
rule.rationale: Hybrid combines push-model velocity for image builds and tag updates with pull-model safety for apply and drift detection. CI never has cluster credentials; the GitOps agent reconciles from Git state.

list.item: Pull advantages: no external cluster credentials, autonomous drift correction, multi-cluster native, audit trail built-in
Push advantages: familiar CI/CD patterns, tight sequencing control, simpler initial setup
Tools (pull): ArgoCD (60% K8s adoption 2025), FluxCD (CNCF graduated), Kargo (multi-env promotion)
Tools (push): GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, Jenkins

section.name: repository-structure
section.p: Repository layout and environment separation strategy is essential for GitOps maintainability and scalability.

rule.statement: Use folder-per-environment, not branch-per-environment, for environment separation.
rule.rationale: Branch-based environments require cross-branch cherry-picks for config changes and make diff visibility hard. Folder-based environments (apps/{app}/overlays/{staging,prod}) keep all environments in one PR diff and enable Kustomize overlays without branching complexity.

example.title: Monorepo folder structure
example.code: gitops-repo/ ├── apps/ │ └── {app-name}/ │ ├── base/ # kustomization.yaml, deployment.yaml, service.yaml │ └── overlays/ │ ├── staging/ # kustomization.yaml with staging-specific patches │ └── production/ ├── infrastructure/ # cert-manager, ingress-nginx, external-secrets ├── clusters/ │ ├── staging/ │ └── production/ └── projects/ └── team-*.yaml # RBAC and project scoping
example.note: Monorepo pattern recommended for organizations with fewer than 50 applications. Larger orgs may use app-per-repo with a central GitOps orchestrator.

section.name: progressive-delivery
section.p: Progressive delivery strategies enable gradual rollouts with automated rollback on metric thresholds.

list.item: Canary: gradually shifts traffic percentage (5% → 25% → 50% → 100%) while analyzing Prometheus metrics at each step; auto-rollback on threshold breach
Blue-Green: runs two identical environments; instant traffic switch after validation; requires 2x resources during deployment window
Argo Rollouts: native controller for canary and blue-green with metric-driven promotion; integrates with ArgoCD
Flagger: progressive delivery controller for FluxCD with Istio/NGINX traffic splitting

section.name: sync-policy-guidelines
section.p: Sync policies define how aggressively the GitOps agent reconciles state and whether it auto-corrects drift.

list.item: Development: auto-sync enabled, self-heal enabled, prune enabled
Staging: auto-sync enabled, self-heal enabled, prune enabled
Production: auto-sync disabled (manual promotion with approval), self-heal enabled, prune disabled or PruneLast

section.name: implementation-checklist
section.p: Ordered checklist from model selection and repository setup through security, application configuration, monitoring, and disaster recovery.

list.item: Evaluate push vs pull vs hybrid model based on security requirements, multi-cluster needs, audit requirements
Select GitOps tool: ArgoCD (UI-heavy, multi-cluster, RBAC/SSO) or FluxCD (lightweight, modular, multi-source)
Evaluate progressive delivery needs: Argo Rollouts (with ArgoCD) or Flagger (with Flux)
Document decision rationale
Create GitOps repository separate from application source code
Choose folder-based environment strategy (not branch-based)
Set up folder structure: apps/, infrastructure/, clusters/, projects/
Configure branch protection: require PR reviews, status checks, no direct pushes to main
Set up CODEOWNERS for team-based approval requirements
Enable signed commits
Add pre-commit validation (OPA/Conftest for policy checks)
Verify Kubernetes cluster running v1.24+, kubectl configured, Helm v3 installed
Create dedicated namespace for GitOps agent
Install with HA for production: multiple replicas, resource limits, PDB configured
Configure GitOps repository authentication: SSH key (recommended) or GitHub App
Verify sync works end-to-end with a test application
Disable default admin account in production
Configure SSO/OIDC integration
Create projects per team with role-based policies
Choose secrets solution: External Secrets Operator, Sealed Secrets, or SOPS
Verify no secrets committed to Git repository
Enable TLS for all connections between GitOps agent and Git/cluster
Enable webhook signature verification
Configure alerts: sync failures, health degradation, drift detected, resource quota
Integrate with log aggregation and set up audit logging
Define maintenance windows and deny windows for production
Document backup procedure for GitOps tool configuration
Test restore process quarterly
Document rollback procedure and test it before first production deployment
